summary: Gruber dissects a Reuters report claiming Apple dropped plans to encrypt iCloud backups after FBI objections. While accepting the core facts — that Apple had such a plan and that the FBI opposed it — he challenges the narrative that Apple proactively briefed the FBI about the plan, arguing this contradicts Apple's fundamental culture of secrecy. He suggests the decision may have been driven by Apple's legal department wanting to avoid political conflict, or by practical concerns about users getting locked out of their data. Regardless of the reason, Gruber calls the lack of encrypted iCloud backups a gaping hole in Apple's privacy commitments and argues encryption should at least be offered as an option.
key_points: iCloud backups lack end-to-end encryption, which contradicts Apple's public identity as the privacy company — many tech-savvy users wrongly assumed their backups were already encrypted

Gruber accepts that Apple had an encryption plan, the FBI opposed it, and Apple shelved it — but disputes that these facts are causally linked, noting Reuters itself could not determine why Apple dropped the plan
Apple proactively briefing the FBI about future product plans would be fundamentally out of character for the company, suggesting the FBI sources may have overstated their role
Apple's legal department may have killed the project to avoid political conflict after the San Bernardino case, or Apple may have been concerned about customers losing access to their own data
The revelation that a 10-person encryption team (code-named Plesio and KeyDrop) was told to stop working is the most alarming detail in the report
money_quotes: I want to go deep on this, because, if true, it's staggering, heartbreaking news.

It simply is not in Apple's nature to tell anyone outside the company about any of its future product plans. I'm not sure how I could make that more clear. It is not in Apple's DNA to ask permission for anything.
I would find it less surprising to know that Apple acquiesced to the FBI's request not to allow encrypted iCloud backups than that Apple briefed the FBI about such a plan before it was put in place.
It does not necessarily follow that #3 is the result of #2.

If that is the case — that Apple's legal department killed the project to avoid 'poking the bear' — then it's ultimately irrelevant whether Apple briefed the FBI in advance or not. It's acquiescence, and users will be left unprotected.
This isn't about Apple foiling law enforcement. It isn't about Apple helping criminals. It's about Apple enabling its customers to own and control their own data.
As things stand, if you use iCloud backup, you do not own and control the data therein.
key_insight: Whether Apple caved to direct FBI pressure or preemptively retreated through its legal department, the result is the same: the biggest hole in Apple's privacy story is that iCloud backups — the only backups most users have — remain accessible to anyone with a court order.
